summary: Nadia responds to Ian Bogost's Atlantic article arguing programmers shouldn't call themselves engineers. She concedes that many modern developers assemble prefabricated code rather than building infrastructure, but argues that a class of true software engineers does exist: those who build and maintain the digital infrastructure everyone else depends on. Using OpenSSL and the Heartbleed bug as her central example, she shows that a tiny team of volunteers maintained code used by two-thirds of the world's web servers. She draws a direct parallel between the pride and responsibility of civil engineers and open source maintainers, arguing that dismissing all software workers ignores those toiling thanklessly beneath the surface.
key_points: Modern software development often involves assembling prefabricated components rather than deep engineering, which partially validates Bogost's claim
Digital infrastructure—developer tools, languages, libraries, frameworks—is as real and consequential as physical infrastructure like bridges
OpenSSL, used by two-thirds of the world's web servers, was maintained by 11 volunteers with less than $2,000/year in donations before Heartbleed
The media stereotype of software developers as overpaid and self-entitled erases the invisible maintainers who make modern programming possible
True software engineers exist precisely because they've made their work invisible—making everyone else's jobs look easy
money_quotes: Digital infrastructure may not be as permanent as physical infrastructure. It may change frequently. But millions of people still depend on digital infrastructure to function.
These tools are actively maintained by volunteers who work relentlessly, without pay, to ensure a programmer's favorite Python http library doesn't break.
Perhaps, if these software engineers make a mistake, people will not die, as with the civil engineers building bridges. But bad things can still happen.
It's become acceptable in tech media to portray software developers as self-entitled, overpaid neckbeards.
To perpetuate these stereotypes ignores those toiling thanklessly under the hood to give those overpaid 'programmers' and 'developers' such a cushy job in the first place.

If Bogost is correct, and today's computer programmers don't really do that much complicated work, then we must acknowledge there is still a quiet class of true 'software engineers' who are working hard to make it easy on the rest of us.
They're making it so easy for us, in fact, that we forget they're even there.
key_insight: The very invisibility of open source infrastructure maintainers—true software engineers—is proof of their success, not evidence of their nonexistence.
